examples.title: Algorithm: Peterson's Mutual Exclusion
examples.description: Ensures mutual exclusion for two processes using flag and turn variables
examples.code:
Algorithm Peterson's Solution
BEGIN
 Shared variables:
 boolean flag[2] = {false, false}
 int turn

 Process P0:
 DO
 flag[0] = true
 turn = 1
 while (flag[1] && turn == 1);
 // Critical Section
 flag[0] = false
 // Remainder Section
 WHILE true

 Process P1:
 DO
 flag[1] = true
 turn = 0
 while (flag[0] && turn == 0);
 // Critical Section
 flag[1] = false
 // Remainder Section
 WHILE true
END
examples.explanation:
1. flag[0/1] = true marks process' desire to enter CS
2. turn = 1/0 gives priority to other process
3. Busy wait until other process exits
4. Reset flag after CS exit
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) per CS entry (busy wait duration depends on contention)

examples.title: C Implementation for Two Threads
examples.description: Peterson's solution implementation using POSIX threads
examples.explanation:
1. Uses volatile variables for shared state
2. Each thread sets its flag and yields turn
3. Busy-wait loop implements entry section
4. printf() represents critical section operations
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for algorithm steps (busy wait not counted in complexity)

examples.title: Peterson's Lock Simulation
examples.description: Python threading implementation showing mutual exclusion pattern
examples.code:
import threading

flag = [False, False]
turn = 0

def process0():
 global flag, turn
 while True:
 flag[0] = True
 turn = 1
 while flag[1] and turn == 1:
 pass
 
 # Critical Section
 print("Process 0 entered CS")
 
 flag[0] = False

def process1():
 global flag, turn
 while True:
 flag[1] = True
 turn = 0
 while flag[0] and turn == 0:
 pass
 
 # Critical Section
 print("Process 1 entered CS")
 
 flag[1] = False

if __name__ == "__main__":
 t0 = threading.Thread(target=process0)
 t1 = threading.Thread(target=process1)
 t0.start()
 t1.start()
examples.explanation:
1. Uses global variables for shared state
2. While loops implement busy waiting
3. print() statements demonstrate CS access
4. Note: Python's GIL limits true parallelism
 but demonstrates algorithm logic
